data.title: RCS - Zenith Energy Ltd - Payment of 2026 multi-currency bond coupon
data.description:
RNS Number : 6128S Zenith Energy Ltd 25 July 2025  

July 25, 2025

ZENITH ENERGY LTD.

("Zenith" or the "Company")

Payment of 2026 multi-currency bond coupon

Zenith Energy Ltd. ("Zenith" or the "Company") (LSE: ZEN; OSE: ZENA; PINK MARKET: ZENAF), the listed international energy production and development company, is pleased to announce that it has recently made coupon payments in full in respect of the following multi-currency Euro Medium Term Notes the Company has issued on the Vienna MTF of the Vienna Stock Exchange:

•     Zenith 2026 (ISIN: XS2835027595)

•     Zenith 2026 (ISIN: XS2796492812)

Further Information:

  • Zenith Energy Ltd

    Andrea Cattaneo, Chief Executive Officer

  • Tel: +1 (587) 315 1279

    E: info@zenithenergy.ca

Notes to Editors:

Zenith Energy Ltd. is a revenue generating, independent energy company with energy production, exploration and development assets in North Africa, the US and Europe. The Company is listed on the London Stock Exchange Main Market , the Euronext Growth of the Oslo Stock Exchange (OSE: ZENA) and the Pink Markets of the OTC (OTC PINK: ZENAF).

Zenith's strategic focus is on pursuing development opportunities through the development of proven revenue generating energy production assets, as well as low-risk exploration activities in assets with existing production.

For more information, please visit: www.zenithenergy.ca

Twitter: @zenithenergyltd

LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/uas/login?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Fcompany%2F10818980%2Fadmin%2F

data.title: U.S. Stock Futures Rise But Asia, European Markets Fall on Rate Cut Uncertainty
data.description:
By Dow Jones Newswires Staff

U.S. stock futures rose after Nasdaq closed at another new record Thursday following Google-parent Alphabet's earnings and despite continued trade tensions and weaker-than-expected housing data. European stocks fell in line with Asia on uncertainty over Fed rate cuts.

  • U.S. futures for the S&P 500 were up 0.1% and futures for the Dow Jones Industrial Average climbed 0.1%. The S&P 500 added less than 0.1% Thursday for its fourth consecutive record, the longest such streak since December while the tech-heavy Nasdaq gained 0.2% to hit its 12th record of July.
  • Asian markets mostly closed lower on profit taking, with Japan's Nikkei down 0.9%. Mainland stocks in China also declined, with Shanghai and ChiNext closing 0.3% and 0.1% lower, respectively. Shenzhen was down 0.1%. In Hong Kong, the Hang Seng fell 1.0%, mainly due to declines in consumer and technology stocks. South Korea's Kospi, however, finished 0.2% higher.
  • European markets were in the red with Germany's DAX down 0.7% and London's FTSE 100 0.3% lower. The Stoxx 600 index was 0.5% lower led by Puma, which fell 17% after the sports brand lowered guidance on tariff-related headwinds. In London, bank NatWest led the premier index risers after raising its full year outlook and saying it plans a 750 million pounds share buyback in the second half-year.
  • The German 10-year Bund yield rose by five basis points to 2.753% from 2.705%, and the yield on the 10-year U.S. Treasury climbed by one basis point to 4.412% from 4.402%. Bond prices and yields move in opposite directions.
  • The Wall Street Journal Dollar Index rose 0.1% to 94.83.
  • Oil prices rose as trade-talk optimism appeared to offset expectations of higher Venezuelan supply. In early trade, Brent crude was up 0.7% to $68.82 a barrel, while WTI was up 0.8% to $66.54 a barrel.
  • Gold futures fell on firmer U.S. jobs data, but is on track for a small overall weekly gain. Futures were down 0.5% at $3,356.60 a troy ounce but were up 0.3% on the week.

Write to Barcelona Editors at barcelonaeditors@dowjones.com

data.title: Eni Has Momentum Going Into the Second Half — Market Talk
data.description: Eni is set for a strong second half of the year and 2026 as its divestment plan and self-help measures deliver results, RBC Capital Markets' Biraj Borkhataria and Adnan Dhanani write. The Italian oil major's balance sheet is more resilient than it has been for many years and free cash flow is growing, they write. Eni reported a decent set of second-quarter results, with a strong performance in its global gas and LNG unit delivering a 5% beat to expectations for group earnings before interest and taxes, they add. Shares trade flat at 14.40 euros. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

data.title: South African assets lose ground in cautious pre-weekend trade
data.description:
South Africa's risk-sensitive rand, stocks and government bonds lost ground on Friday with the possibility of new developments in the United States' trade deal negotiations leaving investors cautious heading into the weekend.

At 0755 GMT, the rand traded at 17.7150 against the dollar , down 0.5% on the day.

It lost about 0.7% on Thursday, weighed down by a stronger U.S. currency and weaker gold price after about a week of gains.

ETM Analytics said the dollar's move higher on Thursday could have triggered "position-squaring ahead of the weekend, where fresh news might break ahead of the Trump administration's tariff deadline" on August 1.

"Investors will not want to hold any significant positions given the U.S. decision-making volatility," the research firm said in a note.

Next week local attention will turn to the South African central bank's interest rate announcement on July 31.

Many traders and analysts expect another rate cut (ZAREPO=ECI) after inflation (ZACPIY=ECI) edged up only modestly in June, moving just inside the central bank's target range.

The Johannesburg Stock Exchange's Top-40 index was down 0.9% in early trade. The benchmark 2035 government bond (ZAR2035=) weakened, as the yield rose six basis points to 9.875%.

data.title: India's Shriram Finance misses profit expectations
data.description:
India's Shriram Finance posted a smaller-than-expected first-quarter profit on Friday.

The non-banking finance company (NBFC) reported an 8.8% year-on-year rise in standalone profit at 21.56 billion rupees ($249.01 million) for the quarter ended June 30.

Analysts, on average, had expected a profit of 21.91 billion rupees, according to data compiled by LSEG.

($1 = 86.5830 Indian rupees)

data.title: Hang Seng Retreats from Near 4-Year High But Up for the Week
data.description:
The Hang Seng Index lost 279 points or 1.1% to close at 25,388 on Friday, snapping a five-session winning streak as traders locked in profits ahead of a key Politburo meeting next week.

Markets pulled back from a near four-year peak amid growing skepticism that Beijing will unveil large-scale stimulus, with attention shifting to U.S.-China trade talks.

Delegations from both countries are set to meet in Stockholm next week to negotiate an extension of the current truce before the August 12 deadline.

Without a deal, tariffs could jump to 145% on U.S. exports and 125% on Chinese goods.

Notable decliners included Kuaishou Tech (-4.9%), Akeso Inc. (-3.3%), Meituan (-3.0%), and Pop Mart Intl. (-2.8%).

Despite the daily loss, the index gained 2.3% for the week—its third straight weekly advance—supported by optimism over a major hydropower project in Tibet and new policy support from Beijing, including a draft amendment to the pricing law aimed at curbing excessive competition and price wars.

data.title: Remy Cointreau CFO: Depletions in the US Are Still Lagging Behind
Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0817 GMT - Eni is set for a strong second half of the year and 2026 as its divestment plan and self-help measures deliver results, RBC Capital Markets' Biraj Borkhataria and Adnan Dhanani write. The Italian oil major's balance sheet is more resilient than it has been for many years and free cash flow is growing, they write. Eni reported a decent set of second-quarter results, with a strong performance in its global gas and LNG unit delivering a 5% beat to expectations for group earnings before interest and taxes, they add. Shares trade flat at 14.40 euros. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0814 GMT - China's plan to rectify a price war in the pharma industry should bode well for the sector, DBS says in a note. In future selections of medical-products suppliers for public hospitals, the lowest bid might not win, China's National Healthcare Security Administration says. DBS thinks this move alleviates the largest risk to China's pharma sector--price cuts. Chinese pharma stocks could have further room to grow as the valuation isn't high from a historical perspective, it says. In view of better industry environment, the market could raise pharma companies' 2H sales and earnings forecasts, it adds. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0811 GMT - Close Brothers' disposal of its Winterflood unit for 104 million pounds in cash provides it with a capital buffer for the car loan probe ruling, Shore Capital says in a research note. "We see this as a decent price for a business that in recent times has been operating around a break-even level due to a combination of cyclical and structural factors, albeit perhaps less good if looked through the lens of historical profitability," analyst Gary Greenwood writes. Any excess capital after the probe is expected to be reinvested in other parts of the business or returned to shareholders, he says. Shares are up 11% at 455 pence. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0810 GMT - NatWest's quarterly print shows that the bank is in a sweet spot after the government returned it to private ownership, Interactive Investor says in a market comment. The U.K. lender is the investor favorite in the sector and its results justify that optimism, analyst Richard Hunter writes. "It remains to be seen whether this new-found freedom will enable a more aggressive acquisition policy," he adds. The bank's significant cash generation brings up the dilemma of whether to boost shareholder returns, invest heavily in the business or pursue an acquisition, he says. The group also raised its returns and income guidance for the year and announced a 750 million pound share buyback, which has been well-received by investors. Shares rise 2% to 513 pence. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0807 GMT - Rightmove's 1H shows its incredible resilience in the face of an uncertain market backdrop, RBC Capital Markets analysts say in a note. The planning delays faced by housebuilders have done little to dent their spending on the property website, RBC says, as average revenue per advertiser grew by 105 pounds for agencies and by 153 pounds in its new-homes division. Although homebuyers hold out for lower mortgage rates, estate agents look keen to increase their spending on the company, RBC says. "Whilst no company can guarantee certainty of results, Rightmove gets closer than most," the analysts say. Shares are down 1% at 786.80 pence and are up 38.5% over the past 12 months.(anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

0750 GMT - Close Brothers Group shares jumped as much as 12% to their highest price since September after the U.K. bank sold its execution services and securities business Winterflood to Marex Group for around 104 million pounds. "This is a positive transaction for CBG, particularly given speculation as recently as April that the business may be sold for only circa 40 million pounds," Keefe, Bruyette & Woods analysts Edward Firth and Elise Yu Ge write in a research note. The deal also simplifies the group and removes a business that brought in considerable levels of uncertainty and volatility over its earnings outlook, they add.(elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0746 GMT - Supalai's capital structure appears positive, Maybank Securities (Thailand)'s Natchaphon Rodjanarowan says in a research report as the brokerage maintains the stock's buy rating. The Thai property developer's net gearing stood at 0.62x at end-1Q, compared with the sector's average of 1.08x, the analyst notes. This low leverage provides Supalai with higher strategic flexibility in navigating an uncertain operating environment. However, the company's tone at a recent analysts' meeting seems negative, with management citing a rising trend in mortgage rejection rates and falling foot traffic at sales sites. The brokerage lowers the stock's target price to THB16.00 from THB24.00 to partly reflect Thailand's subdued macroeconomic outlook. Shares are 1.95% lower at THB15.10. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0741 GMT - Volkswagen earnings show that tariffs are biting as the company lowered its full-year guidance, Bernstein analysts write. Second-quarter results are mildly ahead of consensus, with VW noting an encouraging underlying performance at VW Passenger Cars excluding tariff impacts and restructuring charges. The operating result was 3.0% above Visible Alpha consensus of 3.7 billion euros, Bernstein adds. Audi and Porsche were the biggest underperformers for the quarter, with Audi's operating profit down 64% on year and Porsche's down 91%. The pair's operating profits missed Visible Alpha consensus by 48% and 61%, respectively, the bank says. Shares fall 3.3%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0722 GMT - Coforge has a promising outlook for its 2Q FY 2026 earnings, based on management commentary, Elara Securities (India) analysts say in a research report. The Indian IT services provider's deal with Sabre Corp. is likely to generate meaningful contribution from 2Q, the analysts say. Coforge also maintains its revenue target for FY 2027 of US$2 billion, with medium-term growth expected to be broad-based. Coforge continues to benefit from its strengths such as robust execution capability. The brokerage boosts its FY 2026-2027 revenue estimates by 4%-5% and raises the stock's target price to INR1,850.00 from INR1,770.00 with an unchanged accumulate rating. Shares are 1.0% higher at INR1,692.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0711 GMT - Volkswagen's core brands of VW, Skoda, Seat, Cupra and Volkswagen Commercial Vehicles showed resilience in the second quarter, while the group guidance cut was mostly as expected, Jefferies analysts write. The company posted a resilient second-quarter margin of 4.3% after a 1.2 billion-euro tariff impact in the quarter, they say. The core brands delivered a 6.3% margin from 3.8% last year, while there was the expected poor showings from Audi and Porsche. Audi is due to improve from the third quarter, but Porsche's guidance still looks at risk, the bank adds. As expected, VW reduced guidance to an operating profit margin of 4%-5% and compares with consensus at 4.7%. The working capital outflow is still a weak link, they add. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0706 GMT - Puma's profit warning points to second-quarter sales weakness that is expected to extend into the second half of the year and seems linked to both sectorwide and company-specific challenges, Jefferies analyst say. The German sporting-goods company now expects an EBIT loss for the full year, having previously forecast a positive EBIT of 445 million to 525 million euros, on sales that are expected to decline by a figure in the low-double-digit percentage range excluding currency moves. This is a major profit warning that implies a sales contraction of about 20% for the second half of the year, Jefferies says. The new guidance appears to include net tariff impacts 80 million euros and the cost of addressing inventory increases, according to Jefferies. Shares plummet 18%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0651 GMT - Ningbo Tuopu Group's revenue growth is likely to weaken in 2Q on lower-than-expected sales at key customers including Tesla, Seres and BYD, Jefferies analysts write in a note. The auto-parts supplier's management expects single-digit percentage revenue growth in 2Q, they say. The relatively low content value per vehicle for Geely limited contribution to Tuopu's revenue despite strong volume growth, Jefferies says. Ningbo Tuopu also supplies components for Tesla's robotaxi, with a content value per vehicle of around CNY2,000. The company says that its D&A expenses account for 5% to 7% of revenue, and it expects the figure to approach or exceed 7% when revenue growth slows, they add. Jefferies maintains its buy rating for the stock. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

data.title: Zelenskiy says Ukraine has secured three Patriot systems, looking to finance seven more
data.description:
Ukraine has received confirmation from partners that they will provide three Patriot missile defence systems and discussions are underway to obtain seven more, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said on Thursday.

"I have officially received confirmation from Germany for two systems, and from Norway for one. We are currently working with Dutch partners," he told reporters.

President Donald Trump announced earlier this month that billions of dollars of U.S. weapons would go to Ukraine, including Patriot missiles. This set off negotiations around which partners could contribute to the weapons purchasing scheme — a process that will determine the support Ukraine receives.

The Patriot systems have proven effective at destroying Russian ballistic missiles aimed at Ukraine's cities.

In addition to securing air defence supplies, Ukraine needs to cover a financing gap of $40 billion next year, Zelenskiy said in remarks released by his office.

An additional $25 billion will be needed for missiles, drones and electronic warfare systems production, he added.

data.title: Stocks drop worldwide as investors brace for crucial week
data.description:
Key points:
  • Tariff deadline looms, with Fed, BOJ also meeting
  • Global stocks hit record high this week on trade deal optimism
  • Alphabet earnings buoy Wall Street; Amazon, Apple, Meta due next week

Investors cashed out of highly valued global stocks on Friday and the dollar headed for its biggest weekly drop in a month ahead of a crucial week for markets that includes Donald Trump's tariff deadline and key central bank meetings.

MSCI's global equity index EURONEXT:IACWI retreated from an all-time high and was 0.2% lower in early European trading while Japan's Topix index ended the day 0.9% lower after rising to a record on Thursday.

Europe's STOXX 600 share index also fell 0.5% in early trade.

Ahead of the August 1 deadline for U.S. trade deals with Europe and China, stock markets have been buoyed up by firm U.S. economic data and framed the risk of tariffs hitting growth as a reason to expect Federal Reserve rate cuts.

"Higher (U.S.) inflation will, in time, result in weaker demand and weaker investment," UBS Wealth Management economist Dean Turner said.

Van Luu, head of solutions strategy, fixed income and foreign exchange at Russell Investments, said he was waiting for a buying opportunity in U.S. Treasuries for this reason.

"U.S. data looks astonishingly resilient," he said, but this likely reflected a spending rush before tariffs pushed business input costs and retail sticker prices higher.

RISK EVENTS

The past week saw U.S. trade agreements with Japan, Indonesia and the Philippines, while deal talks continued with South Korea.

Next week brings the next Fed interest rate meeting, the closely watched monthly payrolls report, and earnings from Amazon, Apple, Meta and Microsoft.

Trump has kept up pressure on Fed Chair Jerome Powell to cut rates after a rare presidential visit to the central bank on Thursday, although he said he did not intend to fire Powell, as he has frequently suggested he would.

U.S. 10-year Treasury yields were steady at 4.41% while two-year yields, which track monetary policy bets, were also flat at 3.923%.

Robust earnings from Google parent Alphabet took Wall Street's Nasdaq to a record high on Thursday but futures trading signalled the tech-heavy index would flatline at the start of cash trading in New York.

Contracts tracking the blue-chip S&P 500 index were also flat in early European dealings.

The Bank of Japan has its own policy announcement on Thursday, and Prime Minister Ishiba's Liberal Democratic Party holds a meeting on the same day.

That's after the European Central Bank held rates steady on Thursday, pausing its easing campaign as it waited to assess the impact from U.S. tariffs.

The euro was steady against the dollar on Friday at $1.1745 , although German government debt sold off, with the yield on benchmark 10-year Bunds up 5 basis points (bps) in early dealings to 2.74%, the highest since March 28 (DE10YT=RR).

Japanese government bond yields were steady on Friday at about 1.6%, a level last seen in October 2008, having ratcheted higher on concerns the political scale is tilting more towards fiscal stimulus.

This came after big gains for opposition parties backing consumption tax cuts in Sunday's upper house election. Pressure is building on the more fiscally hawkish Ishiba to quit after his coalition lost its majority in the vote, having done the same in lower house elections last October.

Gold eased 0.3% to around $3,356 an ounce.

Brent crude futures gained 0.7% to $69.65 a barrel.

data.title: Posco Future’s energy materials unit swings to loss
data.description:
Posco Future M Co. (Gyeongsang, South Korea) has reported a 47% year-over-year decline in second-quarter revenue to 506 billion South Korean won ($230 million) for the company’s energy materials business, as per Chemweek.

The unit posted an operating loss of 8.1 billion won, compared with an operating profit of 1.3 billion won in the corresponding period of the previous year.In the cathode subsector, revenue fell 50% year over year to 273.1 billion won. There was a decrease in sales of high-Ni cathode materials on a quarter-over-quarter basis.In the anode subsector, Posco Future M recorded lower sales of 42.3 billion won, down 16% year over year. Sales of NG anodes increased sequentially, primarily driven by demand from overseas customers.Despite the increase in NG anode sales, overall profitability in the cathode subsector deteriorated. This decline was primarily due to lower operating rates. Additionally, the company faced inventory valuation losses.Posco’s other business is basic materials, including refractories, furnace maintenance/construction and quicklime/chemicals.

mrchub.com

data.title: German Business Sentiment Rises Modestly, Misses Expectations
data.description:
The Ifo Business Climate Index for Germany edged up to 88.6 in July 2025, from 88.4 in June, marking the highest reading since May 2024.

However, the result came in slightly below market expectations of 89.0, suggesting that optimism remains cautious.

The improvement was driven by a marginal uptick in both current assessments (86.5 vs 86.2) and business expectations (90.7 vs 90.6), indicating firms are becoming slightly less pessimistic about economic conditions.

data.title: Brigade Hotel Ventures' India IPO fully subscribed on second day of bids
data.description:
Brigade Hotel Ventures' 7.6-billion-rupee ($88 million) initial public offering was fully subscribed on the second day of bidding on Friday, as investor optimism about India's burgeoning hospitality sector strengthens.

Total bids matched the shares on offer, a fresh issue, exchange data showed as of 1:28 p.m. local time.

Shares of the hotel operator, which is aiming for a valuation of around $397 million, are slated to list on July 31, Thursday.

India's hotel operators are cashing in on the surge in travel, as both business and leisure trips rebound across the country. Fueling the momentum is a wave of wealthy Indians spending big on luxury, not just on stays, but on cars, apartments, designer watches and even upscale bathrooms.

Hospitality firms such as ITC Hotels and Leela-owner Schloss Bangalore have made a beeline for public markets over the past two years, betting on sustained demand for premium experiences.

Brigade Hotel Ventures first filed for an IPO in October with an offer size of 9 billion, with the trimmed IPO size coming after a partial debt repayment and pre-IPO fundraises.

The firm runs nine hotels across five Indian cities, partnering with Marriott , Accor and IHG in the mid-to-upscale segments.

($1 = 86.5150 Indian rupees)

data.title: Sharda Cropchem hits record high on quarterly profit jump
data.description:
** Shares of Sharda Cropchem jump 12.8% to a record high of 1,025 rupees

** Agrochemicals company posts fivefold y/y rise in Q1 consol net profit, revenue rises 25%

data.title: China's Plan to Curb Pharma Price War Bodes Well for Sector — Market Talk
data.description: China's plan to rectify a price war in the pharma industry should bode well for the sector, DBS says in a note. In future selections of medical-products suppliers for public hospitals, the lowest bid might not win, China's National Healthcare Security Administration says. DBS thinks this move alleviates the largest risk to China's pharma sector--price cuts. Chinese pharma stocks could have further room to grow as the valuation isn't high from a historical perspective, it says. In view of better industry environment, the market could raise pharma companies' 2H sales and earnings forecasts, it adds. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

data.title: German Business Sentiment Edges Higher Despite Tariff Uncertainty
data.description:
By Ed Frankl

German business confidence inched up this month, a signal of resilience against U.S. tariffs, although with high uncertainty keeping any upswing in check.

The Ifo Institute said Friday that its business-climate index rose to 88.6 in July from 88.4 in June, a little weaker than the 89.0 expected by economists surveyed by The Wall Street Journal.

"The upturn in the German economy remains sluggish," Ifo President Clemens Fuest said.

Companies were slightly more satisfied with current business, though their expectations for future business remained largely unchanged, he added.

Indexes for the manufacturing and construction industries rose in July, but they declined for the services and trade sectors, Ifo said.

Write to Ed Frankl at edward.frankl@wsj.com

data.title: Türkiye imposes duties on packaging film from China, Egypt and Russia
data.description:
Turkiye's Trade Ministry on Wednesday imposed definitive anti-dumping duties on imports of stretchable plastic film used in packaging—technically known as biaxially oriented polypropylene (BOPP) film—from China, Egypt, and Russia, following the outcome of a trade investigation, as per Turkiyetoday.According to the ministry's decision published in the Official Gazette, an anti-dumping investigation into imports of the packaging film from the three countries concluded that the products are sold below their fair market value.The investigation, launched by the ministry in February 2024 following complaints from Turkish producers, finds that these imports cause material injury to Turkiye’s domestic manufacturers by undercutting prices.

As a result of the findings, the ministry decided to implement anti-dumping tariffs on the imported plastic film. For products from China and Egypt, the duty rates will vary by company, ranging between 12.85% and 62.94% of the product’s cost, insurance, and freight (CIF) value. For imports from Russia, a flat rate of 47.14% will apply.The decision aims to restore fair competition in the local market and shield domestic producers from the effects of unfair foreign pricing.

Biaxially Oriented Polypropylene (BOPP) film is a type of plastic film widely used in the packaging industry. It is made by stretching polypropylene—a common plastic resin—in both the machine and transverse directions, which improves its strength, clarity, and barrier properties.BOPP film is commonly used for food packaging, labels, adhesive tapes, and gift wraps due to its lightweight, moisture resistance, and ability to be printed on easily.mrchub.com
